Well, you know, it goes back to if you're doing a diagnostic interview or you're going through. What we want the hot button is to really focus on all the senses. So the better you could do is by touching all the different senses because you don't know what the buyer's, you know, relevancy is. Are they, are they a visual learner? Are they, you know, are they a touch, you know, so if you have a product that you get in their hands and touch and those type of things. So when you're going through all the benefits, you're looking for their responses to find out really what their hot buttons are. If you're reading them, you know, if they're leaning forward, if they're asking more probing questions about certain benefits, you know, that's going to be a hot button for them. So you really want to focus on, on the hot buttons that they really kind of raised their hand over the course of time. You know, should you use all the benefits? I really want to set myself up for success in any sale that I'm going into. So I'm going to use as many benefits as I can without steamrolling over the person to really make the case for the purchase. Dan? Well, so here's what that looks like. It's perfectly okay unless you can do better. So what that looks like is an inverted pyramid and we're starting at the top. So we are presenting 12 benefits. And based on feedback, we are determining that six of them are far more important to you than the other six. Because we come down to pyramid now, we throw six of them pretty much aside, and we talk more about the six we kept. Ultimately, we're determining that two of those six are quote-quote hot buttons for you. By the time we get down to the bottom, to the peak, the narrowest part of the pyramid, and we're about to close, the only thing we're talking about is the two. Okay? That's the process he just described. What's better, if and when it can be done, and often marketing and preparation can help us, is if we can be further along in that pyramid, start narrower based on pre-information. For example, there's very basic pre-information by age bracket, by gender, by married or single, by income, that often allows us to make some of these calculations without having to go through that process. Sometimes, we can get the prospect to tell us those things in advance. If they come to a group presentation out of which they book a one-to-one appointment, if they fill out a questionnaire, at the thing before they come to the, we can design the questionnaire to have them basically rank their hot buttons for us in advance. If they go to a website and they self-select, and there's four choices, they really now pick a shorter, narrower pyramid that we are going to sell in. If you were doing this as a sales presentation, and you did a very simple self-select, I'm a salesperson who sells personally, I'm a sales manager who manages salespeople, and they chose that. You would then be able to start his process in each case at a different spot, focus on fewer hot buttons, still sifting and sorting if we were selling one-on-one, but be more focused in the first place. If we prepare in one-on-one selling, and we do some research, so I'm going to come meet with you if I Google you, if I Wikipedia, if I Facebook you, if I Amazon you, some hot buttons may become apparent. If you go call on somebody in their office, and they have fishing stuff everywhere. First of all, we know that leisure time and recreation is very important to this person, so we probably know that that's a hot button. If I come into your office and there's none of that, it's all business. Then I know that hot button probably isn't there. If I'm going to test it at all, I'm going to test it as a little old by the way, and be off of it.
